parent_section: §4.4 [Linking and Disease Milestones]
verbatim: The timing variables for start and end of a planned assessment interval might be populated for an assessment triggered by a disease milestone, if applicable. For example, the occurrence of a particular event might trigger both a treatment and Holter monitoring for 24 hours after the treatment.

parent_section: §4.4 [Linking and Disease Milestones]
verbatim: Linking and Disease Milestones

parent_section: §4.4 [Linking and Disease Milestones]
verbatim: When disease milestones have been defined for a study, the MIDS variable serves to link observations associated with a disease milestone in a way similar to the way that VISITNUM links observations collected at a visit.

parent_section: §4.4 [Linking and Disease Milestones]
verbatim: If disease milestones were not defined for the study, it would be possible to link records associated with a disease milestone using RELREC, but the use of disease milestones has certain advantages:

parent_section: §4.4 [Linking and Disease Milestones]
verbatim: RELREC indicates that there is a relationship between records or datasets, but not the nature of the relationship. Records with the same MIDS value are related to the same disease milestone.

parent_section: §4.4 [Linking and Disease Milestones]
verbatim: When disease milestones are defined, it is not necessary to create RELREC records to establish relationships between observations associated with a disease milestone.

parent_section: §4.5 [Other Assumptions]
verbatim: 4.5 Other Assumptions

parent_section: §4.5.1 [Original and Standardized Results of Findings and Tests Not Done]
verbatim: 4.5.1 Original and Standardized Results of Findings and Tests Not Done

parent_section: §4.5.1.1 [Original and Standardized Results]
verbatim: 4.5.1.1 Original and Standardized Results

parent_section: §4.5.1.1 [Original and Standardized Results]
verbatim: The --ORRES variable contains the result of the measurement or finding as originally received or collected.

parent_section: §4.5.1.1 [Original and Standardized Results]
verbatim: --ORRES is an expected variable and should always be populated, except (1) when --STAT = "NOT DONE" (because there is no result for such a record) or (2) for derived records.

parent_section: §4.5.1.1 [Original and Standardized Results]
verbatim: NOT DONE

parent_section: §4.5.1.1 [Original and Standardized Results]
verbatim: Note: Records with --DRVFL = "Y" may combine data collected at more than 1 visit. In such cases, sponsors must define the value for VISITNUM, addressing the correct temporal sequence. If a new record is derived for a dataset by the sponsor or their agent (e.g., a CRO), then that new record should be flagged as derived. For example, in electrocardiogram (ECG) data, if a corrected QT interval value derived in-house by the sponsor were represented in an SDTM record, then EGDRVFL would be "Y". If a corrected QT interval value was received from a vendor or was produced by the ECG machine, the derived flag would be null.

parent_section: §4.5.1.1 [Original and Standardized Results]
verbatim: When --ORRES is populated, --STRESС must also be populated, regardless of whether the data values are character or numeric.

parent_section: §4.5.1.1 [Original and Standardized Results]
verbatim: The variable --STRESC is populated either by the conversion of values in --ORRES to values with standard units, or by the assignment of the value of --ORRES, as in the Physical Examination (PE) domain, where --STRESC could contain a dictionary-derived term.

parent_section: §4.5.1.1 [Original and Standardized Results]
verbatim: A further step is necessary when --STRESC contains numeric values. These are converted to numeric type and written to --STRESN.

parent_section: §4.5.1.1 [Original and Standardized Results]
verbatim: Because --STRESC may contain a mixture of numeric and character values, --STRESN may contain null values, as shown in the following figure.

parent_section: §4.5.1.1 [Original and Standardized Results]
verbatim: Figure. Original to Standardized Results

parent_section: §4.5.1.1 [Original and Standardized Results]
verbatim: [FIGURE: Three boxes connected by arrows left-to-right: --ORRES (All original values) → --STRESC (Derive or copy all results) → --STRESN (Numeric results only)]

parent_section: §4.5.1.1 [Original and Standardized Results]
verbatim: When the original measurement or finding is a selection from a defined codelist, in general, the --ORRES and --STRESC variables contain results in decoded format (i.e., the textual interpretation of whichever code was selected from the codelist).

parent_section: §4.5.1.1 [Original and Standardized Results]
verbatim: In some cases where the code values in the codelist are statistically meaningful standardized values or scores, which are defined by sponsors or by valid methodologies such as SF36 questionnaires, the --ORRES variables will contain the decoded format, whereas the --STRESC variables as well as the --STRESN variables will contain the standardized values or scores.

parent_section: §4.5.1.1 [Original and Standardized Results]
verbatim: Occasionally data that are intended to be numeric are collected with characters attached that cause the character-to-numeric conversion to fail.

parent_section: §4.5.1.1 [Original and Standardized Results]
verbatim: For example, numeric cell counts in the source data may be specified with a greater than (>) or less than (<) sign attached (e.g., >10,000, <1). In these cases, the value with the greater than (>) or less than (<) sign attached should be moved to the --STRESC variable, and --STRESN should be null. The rules for [TRUNCATED_AT_PAGE_BOUNDARY]

parent_section: §4.5.1.1 [Original and Standardized Results]
verbatim: modifying the value for analysis purposes should be defined in the analysis plan and a numeric value should only be imputed in the ADaM datasets.

parent_section: §4.5.1.1 [Original and Standardized Results]
verbatim: If the value in --STRESC has different units, the greater than (>) or less than (<) sign should be maintained.

parent_section: §4.5.1.1 [Original and Standardized Results]
verbatim: See Example 1, Rows 11 and 12, in Section 4.5.1.3, Examples of Original and Standard Units and Test Not Done.

parent_section: §4.5.1.2 [Tests Not Done]
verbatim: 4.5.1.2 Tests Not Done

parent_section: §4.5.1.2 [Tests Not Done]
verbatim: If the data on the CRF is missing and "Yes/No" or "Done/Not Done" was not explicitly captured, a record should not be created to indicate that the data was not collected, with the exception of QRS.

parent_section: §4.5.1.2 [Tests Not Done]
verbatim: Regulatory agencies may require a record for all items on a CRF in QRS datasets (e.g., FT, QS, and clinical classifications in RS).

parent_section: §4.5.1.2 [Tests Not Done]
verbatim: If a record is created for a test not done, --REASND is populated only if a reason was explicitly collected except for QRS logically skipped items.

parent_section: §4.5.1.2 [Tests Not Done]
verbatim: When an entire examination (e.g., laboratory draw, ECG, vital signs, physical examination), a group of tests (e.g., hematology, urinalysis), or an individual test (e.g., glucose, PR interval, blood pressure, hearing) is not done, and this information is explicitly captured with a "Yes/No" or "Done/Not Done" question, this information should be represented in the dataset.

parent_section: §4.5.1.2 [Tests Not Done]
verbatim: The reason for the missing information may or may not have been collected.

parent_section: §4.5.1.2 [Tests Not Done]
verbatim: A sponsor has the following options:

parent_section: §4.5.1.2 [Tests Not Done]
verbatim: Submit individual records for each test not done.

parent_section: §4.5.1.2 [Tests Not Done]
verbatim: Submit 1 record for a group of tests that were not done.

parent_section: §4.5.1.2 [Tests Not Done]
verbatim: The following example illustrates the single-record approach for representing a group of tests not done.

parent_section: §4.5.1.2 [Tests Not Done]
verbatim: If a single record is used to represent a group of tests were not done:

parent_section: §4.5.1.2 [Tests Not Done]
verbatim: --TESTCD should be --ALL

parent_section: §4.5.1.2 [Tests Not Done]
verbatim: --TEST should be <Domain description>

parent_section: §4.5.1.2 [Tests Not Done]
verbatim: --CAT should be <Name of group of tests>

parent_section: §4.5.1.2 [Tests Not Done]
verbatim: --ORRES should be null

parent_section: §4.5.1.2 [Tests Not Done]
verbatim: --STAT should be "NOT DONE"

parent_section: §4.5.1.2 [Tests Not Done]
verbatim: NOT DONE

parent_section: §4.5.1.2 [Tests Not Done]
verbatim: --REASND, if collected, might be "Specimen lost"

parent_section: §4.5.1.2 [Tests Not Done]
verbatim: For example, if urinalysis tests were not done, then:

parent_section: §4.5.1.2 [Tests Not Done]
verbatim: LBTESTCD would be "LBALL"

parent_section: §4.5.1.2 [Tests Not Done]
verbatim: LBALL

parent_section: §4.5.1.2 [Tests Not Done]
verbatim: LBTEST would be "Laboratory Test Results"

parent_section: §4.5.1.2 [Tests Not Done]
verbatim: LBCAT would be "URINALYSIS"

parent_section: §4.5.1.2 [Tests Not Done]
verbatim: URINALYSIS

parent_section: §4.5.1.2 [Tests Not Done]
verbatim: LBORRES would be null

parent_section: §4.5.1.2 [Tests Not Done]
verbatim: LBSTAT would be "NOT DONE"

parent_section: §4.5.1.2 [Tests Not Done]
verbatim: NOT DONE

parent_section: §4.5.1.2 [Tests Not Done]
verbatim: LBREASND, if collected, might be "Subject could not void"

parent_section: §4.5.1.3 [Examples of Original and Standard Units and Test Not Done]
verbatim: 4.5.1.3 Examples of Original and Standard Units and Test Not Done

parent_section: §4.5.1.3 [Examples of Original and Standard Units and Test Not Done]
verbatim: The following examples are meant to illustrate the use of Findings results variables, and are not meant as comprehensive domain examples.

parent_section: §4.5.1.3 [Examples of Original and Standard Units and Test Not Done]
verbatim: Certain required and expected variables are omitted (e.g., USUBJID), and the samples may represent data for more than 1 subject.

parent_section: §4.5.1.3 [Examples of Original and Standard Units and Test Not Done]
verbatim: Example 1

parent_section: §4.5.1.3 [Examples of Original and Standard Units and Test Not Done]
verbatim: Row 1: A numeric value was converted to the standard unit.

parent_section: §4.5.1.3 [Examples of Original and Standard Units and Test Not Done]
verbatim: Row 2: A numeric value was copied; the original unit was the standard unit so conversion was not needed.

parent_section: §4.5.1.3 [Examples of Original and Standard Units and Test Not Done]
verbatim: Rows 3-4: A character result was copied from the LBORRES to LBSTRESC. Since this is not a numeric result, LBSTRESN is null.

parent_section: §4.5.1.3 [Examples of Original and Standard Units and Test Not Done]
verbatim: Row 5: A character result was converted to a standardized format.

parent_section: §4.5.1.3 [Examples of Original and Standard Units and Test Not Done]
verbatim: Row 6: A result of "BLQ" was collected and copied to LBSTRESC. Note that the sponsor populated both LBORRESU and LBSTRESU with standard units, but these could have been left null.

parent_section: §4.5.1.3 [Examples of Original and Standard Units and Test Not Done]
verbatim: Row 7: A result was derived from multiple results, so LBDRVFL = "Y". Note that the original collected data are not shown in this example.

parent_section: §4.5.1.3 [Examples of Original and Standard Units and Test Not Done]
verbatim: Row 8: A result for LBTEST = "HCT" is missing for visit 2, as indicated by LBSTAT = "NOT DONE"; neither LBORRES nor LBSTRESC is populated.

parent_section: §4.5.1.3 [Examples of Original and Standard Units and Test Not Done]
verbatim: Row 9: Tests in the category "HEMATOLOGY" were not done at visit 3, as indicated by LBTESTCD = "LBALL" and LBSTAT = "NOT DONE".

parent_section: §4.5.1.3 [Examples of Original and Standard Units and Test Not Done]
verbatim: Row 10: None of the tests in the LB domain were done at visit 4, as indicated by LBTESTCD = "LBALL", a null LBCAT value, and LBSTAT = "NOT DONE".

parent_section: §4.5.1.3 [Examples of Original and Standard Units and Test Not Done]
verbatim: Row 11: Shows a result collected as an inequality. The unit collected was the standard unit, so the result required no conversion and was copied to LBSTRESC.

parent_section: §4.5.1.3 [Examples of Original and Standard Units and Test Not Done]
verbatim: Row 12: Shows a result collected as an inequality. In LBSTRESC, the numeric part of LBORRES has been converted to the standard unit, and the less than (<) sign has been retained. LBSTRESN is not populated.

§4.5.2 [Linking Multiple Observations]
verbatim: 4.5.2 Linking Multiple Observations

parent_section: §4.5.2 [Linking Multiple Observations]
verbatim: See Section 8, Representing Relationships and Data, for guidance on expressing relationships among multiple observations.

parent_section: §4.5.3 [Text Strings that Exceed the Maximum Length for General Observation-class Domain Variables]
verbatim: 4.5.3 Text Strings that Exceed the Maximum Length for General Observation-class Domain Variables

parent_section: §4.5.3.1 [Test Name (--TEST) Greater than 40 Characters]
verbatim: 4.5.3.1 Test Name (--TEST) Greater than 40 Characters

parent_section: §4.5.3.1 [Test Name (--TEST) Greater than 40 Characters]
verbatim: Sponsors may have test descriptions (--TEST) longer than 40 characters in their operational database.

parent_section: §4.5.3.1 [Test Name (--TEST) Greater than 40 Characters]
verbatim: Because the --TEST variable is meant to serve as a label for a --TESTCD when a Findings dataset is transposed to a more horizontal format, the length of --TEST is limited to 40 characters (except as noted below) to conform to the limitations of the SAS V5 transport file format (https://documentation.sas.com/).

parent_section: §4.5.3.1 [Test Name (--TEST) Greater than 40 Characters]
verbatim: Therefore, sponsors have the choice to either insert the first 40 characters or a text string abbreviated to 40 characters in --TEST.

parent_section: §4.5.3.1 [Test Name (--TEST) Greater than 40 Characters]
verbatim: Sponsors have the following options for including the full description for these variables in the study metadata:

parent_section: §4.5.3.1 [Test Name (--TEST) Greater than 40 Characters]
verbatim: If the annotated CRF contains the full text, provide a reference to the aCRF page containing the full test description in the Define-XML document origin definition for --TEST.

parent_section: §4.5.3.1 [Test Name (--TEST) Greater than 40 Characters]
verbatim: If the annotated CRF does not specify the full text, then the full text should be documented in the Define-XML document and/or other submission materials (e.g., the clinical study data reviewer's guide).

parent_section: §4.5.3.1 [Test Name (--TEST) Greater than 40 Characters]
verbatim: This convention should also be applied to the qualifier value label (QLABEL) in Supplemental Qualifiers (SUPP--) datasets.

parent_section: §4.5.3.1 [Test Name (--TEST) Greater than 40 Characters]
verbatim: IETEST values in the Inclusion/Exclusion Criteria Not Met (IE) and Trial Inclusion/Exclusion Criteria (TI) domains are exceptions to the 40-character rule and are limited to 200 characters, because these are not expected to be transformed to column labels.

parent_section: §4.5.3.1 [Test Name (--TEST) Greater than 40 Characters]
verbatim: Values of IETEST that exceed 200 characters should be described in study metadata as per the convention above.

parent_section: §4.5.3.1 [Test Name (--TEST) Greater than 40 Characters]
verbatim: See Section 6.3.4, Inclusion/Exclusion Criteria Not Met, assumption 3; and Section 7.4.1, Trial Inclusion/Exclusion Criteria, assumption 5.

parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]
verbatim: 4.5.3.2 Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables

parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]
verbatim: Some sponsors may collect data values longer than 200 characters for some variables.

parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]
verbatim: Because of the current requirement for the SAS V5 transport file format, it is not possible to store long text strings using only 1 variable.

parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]
verbatim: Therefore, the SDTMIG has defined conventions for storing long text strings using multiple variables.

parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]
verbatim: For general observation-class variables and supplemental qualifiers (i.e., non-standard variables, NSVs), the conventions are as follows:

parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]
verbatim: The first 200 characters of text should be stored in the parent domain variable and each additional 200 characters of text should be stored in a record in the SUPP-- dataset (see Section 8.4, Relating Non-standard Variable Values to a Parent Domain).

parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]
verbatim: When splitting a text string into several records, the text should be split between words to improve readability.

parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]
verbatim: When the text longer than 200 characters is for a supplemental qualifier, the first QNAM should describe the NSV without any numeric suffix.

parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]
verbatim: The value for QNAMs for additional text (>200 characters) should contain a sequential variable name, which is formed by appending a 1-digit integer, beginning with 1, to the original domain variable name.

parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]
verbatim: The value for QLABEL should be the original domain variable label.

parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]
verbatim: The reason a digit integer or suffix is not appended to the label is because the long text string represents a single value for a variable. The physical representation (i.e., SAS V5 transport file format) does not change the concept described by the label.

parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]
verbatim: This is different conceptually from when there are multiple values for a non-result qualifier variable and values are individually stored in SUPP--. In that case, both QNAM and QLABEL must be uniquely named (see Section 4.2.8.3, Multiple Values for a Non-result Qualifier Variable) because they represent multiple values for a single variable.

parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]
verbatim: In cases where the standard domain variable name is already 8 characters in length, sponsors should replace the last character with a digit when creating values for QNAM. As an example, for Other Action Taken in Adverse Events (AEACNOTH), values for QNAM for the SUPPAE records would have the values AEACNOT1, AEACNOT2, and so on.

parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]
verbatim: AEACNOT1

parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]
verbatim: AEACNOT2

parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]
verbatim: Example 1

parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]
verbatim: In this example, the text entered for MHTERM was longer than 200 characters and required 2 supplemental qualifier variables for the text that extended beyond what could be represented in the standard variable.

parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]
verbatim: mh.xpt

verbatim: Row | STUDYID | DOMAIN | USUBJID | MHSEQ | MHTERM

parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]
verbatim: 1 | 12345 | MH | 99-123 | 6 | 1st ~200 chars of text, split between words

verbatim: suppmh.xpt

verbatim: Row | STUDYID | RDOMAIN | USUBJID | IDVAR | IDVARVAL | QNAM | QLABEL | QVAL | QORIG | QEVAL

parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]
verbatim: 2 | 12345 | MH | 99-123 | MHSEQ | 6 | MHTERM2 | Reported Term for the Medical History | last 100 or more chars of text | CRF | |

parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]
verbatim: MHTERM1

parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]
verbatim: MHTERM2

parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]
verbatim: Example 2

parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]
verbatim: In this example, the text entered for AEACNOTH was longer than 200 characters, but required only 1 supplemental qualifier for the text that extended beyond what could be represented in the standard variable.

parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]
verbatim: The first 200 characters of text should be stored in the variable and separate additional 200 characters of text in supplemental qualifiers.

parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]
verbatim: When splitting a text string into several records, the text should be split between words to improve readability.

parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]
verbatim: The value for QLABEL should be the original domain variable label.

parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]
verbatim: Text Strings >200 Char Conventions for General Supplemental Qualifier Variables | Text Strings >200 Char Conventions for COVAL and Related Columns | Text Strings >200 Char Conventions for TSVAL and Related Columns | Text Strings >200 Char Conventions for IETEST

parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]
verbatim: The first 200 characters of text should be stored in the variable and separate additional 200 characters of text in the SUPP-- dataset | The first 200 characters of text should be stored in COVAL and each additional 200 characters of text stored in additional columns COVAL+ | The first 200 characters of text should be stored in TSVAL and each additional 200 characters of text stored in additional columns TSVALn | If the inclusion/exclusion criteria text is >200 characters, put meaningful text in IETEST and describe the full text of the criteria, if appropriate

parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]
verbatim: When splitting a text string into several records, the text should be split between words to improve readability | When splitting a text string into several records, the text should be split between words | When splitting a text string into several records, the text should be split between words | Not applicable

parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]
verbatim: The value for QLABEL should be the original domain variable label | The variable labels for COVAL+ to COVALn should be "Comment" | The variable labels for TSVAL+ to TSVALn should be "Parameter Value" | Not applicable

parent_section: §4.5.4 [Evaluators in the Interventions and Events Observation Classes]
verbatim: 4.5.4 Evaluators in the Interventions and Events Observation Classes

parent_section: §4.5.4 [Evaluators in the Interventions and Events Observation Classes]
verbatim: Because observations may originate from more than 1 source (e.g., investigator, independent assessor), observations recorded in the Findings class include the --EVAL qualifier.

parent_section: §4.5.4 [Evaluators in the Interventions and Events Observation Classes]
verbatim: For the Interventions and Events observation classes, which do not include the --EVAL variable, all data are assumed to be attributed to the principal investigator.

parent_section: §4.5.4 [Evaluators in the Interventions and Events Observation Classes]
verbatim: The QEVAL variable can be used to describe the evaluator for any data item in a SUPP-- dataset.

parent_section: §4.5.4 [Evaluators in the Interventions and Events Observation Classes]
verbatim: See Section 8.4.1, Supplemental Qualifiers—SUPP-- Datasets for additional detail.

parent_section: §4.5.4 [Evaluators in the Interventions and Events Observation Classes]
verbatim: This example illustrates a case where an adjudication committee evaluated an adverse event.

parent_section: §4.5.4 [Evaluators in the Interventions and Events Observation Classes]
verbatim: The evaluations of the adverse event by the primary investigator were represented in the standard AE dataset.

parent_section: §4.5.4 [Evaluators in the Interventions and Events Observation Classes]
verbatim: The evaluations of the adjudication committee were represented in SUPPAE.

parent_section: §4.5.4 [Evaluators in the Interventions and Events Observation Classes]
verbatim: See Section 8.4, Relating Non-standard Variable Values to a Datasets.

parent_section: §4.5.5 [Clinical Significance for Findings Observation Class Data]
verbatim: 4.5.5 Clinical Significance for Findings Observation Class Data

parent_section: §4.5.5 [Clinical Significance for Findings Observation Class Data]
verbatim: For assessments of clinical significance when the overall interpretation is a record in the domain, use the --CLSIG (Clinically Significant) variable on the record that contains the overall interpretation or a particular result.

parent_section: §4.5.5 [Clinical Significance for Findings Observation Class Data]
verbatim: For example, EGCLSIG = "Y" indicates that an ECG result of "ATRIAL FIBRILLATION" was clinically significant.

parent_section: §4.5.5 [Clinical Significance for Findings Observation Class Data]
verbatim: Separate from clinical significance are results of "NORMAL" or "ABNORMAL", or lab values that are out of normal range.

parent_section: §4.5.5 [Clinical Significance for Findings Observation Class Data]
verbatim: An ECG test with EGTESTCD = "INTP" (which addresses the ECG as a whole) should have a result of or "NORMAL" or "ABNORMAL". A record for EGTESTCD = "INTP" may also have EGCLSIG indicating whether the result is clinically significant.

parent_section: §4.5.5 [Clinical Significance for Findings Observation Class Data]
verbatim: A record for a vital signs measurement (e.g., systolic blood pressure) or a lab test (e.g., hematocrit) that contains a measurement may have a normal range and a normal range indicator. It could also have --CLSIG indicating whether the result is clinically significant.

parent_section: §4.5.6 [Supplemental Reason Variables]
verbatim: 4.5.6 Supplemental Reason Variables

parent_section: §4.5.6 [Supplemental Reason Variables]
verbatim: The SDTM general observation classes include the --REASND variable to submit the reason a response is not present (a result in a findings class or an --OCCUR value in an events or interventions variable).

parent_section: §4.5.6 [Supplemental Reason Variables]
verbatim: For Events and Interventions domains where specified occurrences have a reason for the "Y" or "N" value of --OCCUR, the reason can be represented with the variable --REASOC.

parent_section: §4.5.6 [Supplemental Reason Variables]
verbatim: However, sponsors sometimes collect the reason that something was done.

parent_section: §4.5.6 [Supplemental Reason Variables]
verbatim: For the Interventions general observation class, --INDC is available to represent the medical condition for which the intervention was given, and --ADJ is available to represent the reason for a dose adjustment.

parent_section: §4.5.6 [Supplemental Reason Variables]
verbatim: For the Findings general observation class, --REASPE is available to represent the reason a test was performed.

parent_section: §4.5.6 [Supplemental Reason Variables]
verbatim: If the sponsor collects a reason for performing an activity represented in an Events domain where the topic is not a medical condition, or a reason for an intervention other than a medical indication, the reason can be represented in the SUPP-- dataset (as described in Section 8.4.1, Supplemental Qualifiers—SUPP--Datasets) using the supplemental qualifier with QNAM of "--REAS" listed in Appendix C1, Supplemental Qualifiers Name Codes.

parent_section: §4.5.6 [Supplemental Reason Variables]
verbatim: If multiple reasons are reported, refer to Section 4.2.8.3, Multiple Values for a Non-result Qualifier Variable.

parent_section: §4.5.7 [Presence or Absence of Prespecified Interventions and Events]
verbatim: 4.5.7 Presence or Absence of Prespecified Interventions and Events

parent_section: §4.5.7 [Presence or Absence of Prespecified Interventions and Events]
verbatim: Interventions (e.g., concomitant medications) and events (e.g., medical history) can generally be collected in 2 different ways, by recording either verbatim free text or the responses to a prespecified list of treatments or terms.

parent_section: §4.5.7 [Presence or Absence of Prespecified Interventions and Events]
verbatim: Because the method of solicitation for information on treatments and terms may affect the frequency at which they are reported, whether they were prespecified or not may be of interest to reviewers.

parent_section: §4.5.7 [Presence or Absence of Prespecified Interventions and Events]
verbatim: The --PRESP variable is used to indicate whether a specific intervention (--TRT) or event (--TERM) was solicited.

parent_section: §4.5.7 [Presence or Absence of Prespecified Interventions and Events]
verbatim: The --PRESP variable has controlled terminology of "Y" (for "Yes") or a null value.

parent_section: §4.5.7 [Presence or Absence of Prespecified Interventions and Events]
verbatim: It is a permissible variable, and should only be used when the topic variable values come from a prespecified list.

parent_section: §4.5.7 [Presence or Absence of Prespecified Interventions and Events]
verbatim: Questions such as "Did the subject have any concomitant medications?" or "Did the subject have any medical history?" should not have records in an SDTM domain because these are not valid values for the topic variable.

parent_section: §4.5.7 [Presence or Absence of Prespecified Interventions and Events]
verbatim: (1) these are not valid values for the respective topic variables of CMTRT and MHTERM, and (2) records whose sole purpose is to indicate whether or not a subject had records are not meaningful.

parent_section: §4.5.7 [Presence or Absence of Prespecified Interventions and Events]
verbatim: The --OCCUR variable is used to indicate whether a prespecified intervention or event occurred or did not occur.

parent_section: §4.5.7 [Presence or Absence of Prespecified Interventions and Events]
verbatim: It has controlled terminology of "Y" and "N" (for "Yes" and "No").

parent_section: §4.5.7 [Presence or Absence of Prespecified Interventions and Events]
verbatim: It is a permissible variable and may be omitted from the dataset if no topic-variable values were prespecified.

parent_section: §4.5.7 [Presence or Absence of Prespecified Interventions and Events]
verbatim: If a study collects both prespecified interventions and events as well as free-text events and interventions, the value of --OCCUR should be "Y" or "N" for all prespecified interventions and events, and null for those reported as free text.

parent_section: §4.5.7 [Presence or Absence of Prespecified Interventions and Events]
verbatim: Situation Probably reported event occurred | Value of --PRESP | Value of --OCCUR | Value of --STAT

parent_section: §4.5.7 [Presence or Absence of Prespecified Interventions and Events]
verbatim: Spontaneously reported event occurred | Y | Y |

parent_section: §4.5.7 [Presence or Absence of Prespecified Interventions and Events]
verbatim: Prespecified event occurred | Y | Y |

parent_section: §4.5.7 [Presence or Absence of Prespecified Interventions and Events]
verbatim: Prespecified event did not occur | Y | N |

parent_section: §4.5.7 [Presence or Absence of Prespecified Interventions and Events]
verbatim: Prespecified event no response | Y |  | NOT DONE

parent_section: §4.5.7 [Presence or Absence of Prespecified Interventions and Events]
verbatim: Collection design may prespecify specific treatments or terms or prespecify a group of treatments or terms (e.g., "Was a short-acting bronchodilator taken in the 8 hours prior to spirometry?").

parent_section: §4.5.7 [Presence or Absence of Prespecified Interventions and Events]
verbatim: When an explicit question asks about the occurrence of a group of interventions or events, the group value should be represented in --TRT or --TERM, respectively.

parent_section: §4.5.7 [Presence or Absence of Prespecified Interventions and Events]
verbatim: Refer to the standard domains in the Events and Interventions general observation classes for additional assumptions and examples.

parent_section: §4.5.8 [Accounting for Long-term Follow-up]
verbatim: 4.5.8 Accounting for Long-term Follow-up

parent_section: §4.5.8 [Accounting for Long-term Follow-up]
verbatim: Studies often include long-term follow-up assessments to monitor a subject's condition.

parent_section: §4.5.8 [Accounting for Long-term Follow-up]
verbatim: Use cases include studies in terminally ill populations that periodically assess survival and studies involving chronic disease that include follow-up to assess relapse.

parent_section: §4.5.8 [Accounting for Long-term Follow-up]
verbatim: Long-term follow-up is often conducted via telephone calls rather than clinic visits.

parent_section: §4.5.8 [Accounting for Long-term Follow-up]
verbatim: Regardless of the method of collection of the information, the data should be stored in the appropriate special domain.

parent_section: §4.5.8 [Accounting for Long-term Follow-up]
verbatim: Overall study conclusion in the Disposition (DS) domain occurs once all contact with the subject ceases.

parent_section: §4.5.8 [Accounting for Long-term Follow-up]
verbatim: If a study has a clinical treatment phase followed by a long-term follow-up phase, these 2 segments of the study can be represented as separate epochs within the overall study, each with its own epoch disposition record.

parent_section: §4.5.8 [Accounting for Long-term Follow-up]
verbatim: The following example illustrates the recommended SDTM approach to storing these data.

parent_section: §4.5.8 [Accounting for Long-term Follow-up]
verbatim: An oncology study encompasses 2 months of clinical treatment and assessments followed by once-monthly telephone contacts.

parent_section: §4.5.8 [Accounting for Long-term Follow-up]
verbatim: The contacts continue until the subject ceases or stops.

parent_section: §4.5.8 [Accounting for Long-term Follow-up]
verbatim: During the telephone contacts, the investigator collects information on the subject's survival status and medication use.

parent_section: §4.5.8 [Accounting for Long-term Follow-up]
verbatim: The answers to certain questions may trigger other data collection.

parent_section: §4.5.8 [Accounting for Long-term Follow-up]
verbatim: For example, if the subject's survival status is "dead", then this indicates that the subject has ceased participation in the study, so a study discontinuation record would need to be created.

parent_section: §4.5.8 [Accounting for Long-term Follow-up]
verbatim: In SDTM, the data related to these follow-up telephone contacts should be stored as follows:

parent_section: §4.5.8 [Accounting for Long-term Follow-up]
verbatim: Concomitant medications reported during the contact should be stored in the CM domain.

parent_section: §4.5.8 [Accounting for Long-term Follow-up]
verbatim: The subject's survival status should be stored in the Subject Status (SS) domain.

parent_section: §4.5.8 [Accounting for Long-term Follow-up]
verbatim: The disposition of the subject at the time of the final follow-up contact should be stored in DS. Note that overall study conclusion is the point where any contact with the subject ceases, which in this example is also the conclusion of long-term follow-up.

parent_section: §4.5.8 [Accounting for Long-term Follow-up]
verbatim: The disposition of the subject at the conclusion of the clinical treatment phase would be stored in DS as the conclusion to that epoch.

parent_section: §4.5.8 [Accounting for Long-term Follow-up]
verbatim: Long-term follow-up would be represented as a separate epoch.

parent_section: §4.5.8 [Accounting for Long-term Follow-up]
verbatim: Therefore, in this example the subject could have 3 disposition records in DS, with the following-up epoch disposition and the overall study conclusion disposition being collected at the final telephone contact.

parent_section: §4.5.8 [Accounting for Long-term Follow-up]
verbatim: If the subject's survival status is "dead", the Demographics (DM) variables DTHDT and DTHFL must be appropriately populated.

parent_section: §4.5.9 [Baseline Values]
verbatim: 4.5.9 Baseline Values

parent_section: §4.5.9 [Baseline Values]
verbatim: The variable --LOBXFL was introduced in SDTMIG v3.3 to address the need for a consistent definition of a value that can serve as a reference with which to compare post-treatment values.

parent_section: §4.5.9 [Baseline Values]
verbatim: This generic baseline definition approximates the concept of baseline and can be used to calculate post-treatment changes.

parent_section: §4.5.9 [Baseline Values]
verbatim: In domains where --BLFL was expected, its core value was changed from expected to permissible and the variable --LOBXFL with a core value of expected, was added to contain the consistent definition.

parent_section: §4.5.9 [Baseline Values]
verbatim: In domains where --BLFL was permissible, the variable --LOBXFL was added with a core value of permissible.

parent_section: §4.5.9 [Baseline Values]
verbatim: Variable Name | Structure Where It is Defined | Requirement in That Structure | Definition | Intended Use

parent_section: §4.5.9 [Baseline Values]
verbatim: --LOBXFL | SDTM Findings | Expected or Permissible | Last non-missing value prior to RFXSTDTC (operationally derived) | Consistent pre-treatment reference value baseline for use across all studies and sponsors

parent_section: §4.5.9 [Baseline Values]
verbatim: ABLFL | ADaM BDS | Conditionally Required | Flags the record that is the source of the baseline value for a given parameter specified in the statistical analysis plan (SAP; may differ both across and within studies and datasets) | Baseline for ADaM analysis as specified in the SAP

parent_section: §4.5.9 [Baseline Values]
verbatim: --BLFL | SDTM Findings | Permissible (formerly Expected in some domains) | A baseline defined by the sponsor (could be derived in the same manner as --LOBXFL or --BE, but is required to be) | Any sponsor-defined baseline use

parent_section: §4.5.9 [Baseline Values]
verbatim: As shown in the table, each variable serves a specific need.

parent_section: §4.5.9 [Baseline Values]
verbatim: The SDTM variable --LOBXFL (and/or --BLFL, if used) can be copied to ADaM for traceability and transparency, but only the ADaM variable ABLFL would be used to signify baseline for analysis.

parent_section: §4.5.9 [Baseline Values]
verbatim: The content of --LOBXFL and ABLFL will be exactly the same when the SAP specifies that the baseline used for analysis is the last non-missing value prior to RFXSTDTC.

parent_section: §5 [Models for Special-purpose Domains]
verbatim: 5  Models for Special-purpose Domains

parent_section: §5 [Models for Special-purpose Domains]
verbatim: Special-purpose Domains is an SDTM class in its own right.

parent_section: §5 [Models for Special-purpose Domains]
verbatim: Special-purpose domains provide specific, standardized structures to represent additional important information that does not fit any of the general observation classes.

parent_section: §5.1 [Comments (CO)]
verbatim: 5.1 Comments (CO)

parent_section: §5.1 [Comments (CO)]
verbatim: CO – Description/Overview

parent_section: §5.1 [Comments (CO)]
verbatim: A special-purpose domain that contains comments that may be collected alongside other data.

parent_section: §5.1 [Comments (CO)]
verbatim: CO – Specification

parent_section: §5.1 [Comments (CO)]
verbatim: co.xpt, Comments — Special Purpose. One record per comment per subject, Tabulation.

parent_section: §5.1 [Comments (CO)]
verbatim: co.xpt

parent_section: §5.1 [Comments (CO)]
verbatim: | Variable Name | Variable Label | Type | Controlled Terms, Codelist or Format¹ | Role | CDISC Notes | Core |

parent_section: §5.1 [Comments (CO)]
verbatim: | STUDYID | Study Identifier | Char | | Identifier | Unique identifier for a study. | Req |

parent_section: §5.1 [Comments (CO)]
verbatim: | DOMAIN | Domain Abbreviation | Char | CO | Identifier | Two-character abbreviation for the domain. | Req |

parent_section: §5.1 [Comments (CO)]
verbatim: | RDOMAIN | Related Domain Abbreviation | Char | (DOMAIN) | Record Qualifier | Two-character abbreviation for the domain of the parent record(s). Null for comments collected on a general comments or additional information CRF page. | Perm |

parent_section: §5.1 [Comments (CO)]
verbatim: | USUBJID | Unique Subject Identifier | Char | | Identifier | Identifier used to uniquely identify a subject across all studies for all applications or submissions involving the product. | Req |

parent_section: §5.1 [Comments (CO)]
verbatim: | COSEQ | Sequence Number | Num | | Identifier | Sequence Number given to ensure uniqueness of subject records within a domain. May be any valid number. | Req |

parent_section: §5.1 [Comments (CO)]
verbatim: | IDVAR | Identifying Variable | Char | * | Record Qualifier | Identifying variable in the parent dataset that identifies the record(s) to which the comment applies. Examples AESEQ or CMGRPID. Used only when individual comments are related to domain records. Null for comments collected on separate CRFs. | Perm |

parent_section: §5.1 [Comments (CO)]
verbatim: | IDVARVAL | Identifying Variable Value | Char | | Record Qualifier | Value of identifying variable of the parent record(s). Used only when individual comments are related to domain records. Null for comments collected on separate CRFs. | Perm |

parent_section: §5.1 [Comments (CO)]
verbatim: | COREF | Comment Reference | Char | | Record Qualifier | Sponsor-defined reference associated with the comment. May be the CRF page number (e.g., 650), or a module name (e.g., DEMOG), or a combination of information that identifies the reference (e.g. 650-VITALS-VISIT 2). | Perm |

parent_section: §5.1 [Comments (CO)]
verbatim: | COVAL | Comment | Char | | Topic | The text of the comment. Text over 200 characters can be added to additional columns COVAL1-COVALn. See Assumption 3. | Req |

parent_section: §5.1 [Comments (CO)]
verbatim: | COEVAL | Evaluator | Char | (EVAL) | Record Qualifier | Role of the person who provided the evaluation. Used only for results that are subjective (e.g., assigned by a person or a group). Example: “INVESTIGATOR”. | Perm |

parent_section: §5.1 [Comments (CO)]
verbatim: | COEVALID | Evaluator Identifier | Char | (MEDEVAL) | Record Qualifier | Used to distinguish multiple evaluators with the same role recorded in --EVAL. Examples: “RADIOLOGIST”, “RADIOLOGIST 1”, “RADIOLOGIST 2”. | Perm |

parent_section: §5.1 [Comments (CO)]
verbatim: | CODTC | Date/Time of Comment | Char | ISO 8601 datetime or interval | Timing | Date/time of comment on dedicated comment form. Should be null if this is a child record of another domain or if comment date was not collected. | Perm |

parent_section: §5.1 [Comments (CO)]
verbatim: | CODY | Study Day of Comment | Num | | Timing | Study day of the comment, in integer days. The algorithm for calculations must be relative to the sponsor-defined RFSTDTC variable in the Demographics (DM) domain. | Perm |

parent_section: §5.1 [Comments (CO)]
verbatim: ¹In this column, an asterisk (*) indicates that the variable may be subject to controlled terminology. CDISC/NCI codelist values are enclosed in parentheses.
